channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: One Ring Magic: The Gathering Artist Apologizes After Fans Discover Plagiarism: ‘I Made A Mistake And I Feel Awful’
item.content:encoded:
After several days of speculation from fans and fellow artists alike, Wizards of the Coast has released a statement confirming that the recently revealed The One Ring artwork, which is set to release in the upcoming Magic: The Gathering set The Hobbit (Universes Beyond), was “unfortunately” plagiarized by renowned MtG artist Dan Frazier.

The controversy surrounding The One Ring artwork has been rapidly building for a few days now, bolstered in part by this thread on the r/magicTCG subreddit. It’s pretty obvious when you put the cards side by side, but Dan Frazier’s artwork (on the left) for the card from the new The Hobbit set bears a striking resemblance to Marta Neal’s version (on the right) from the Tales of Middle-earth set.

Once you flip Neal’s artwork, it’s easy to see. In fact, the runes from Neal’s version of the card can still be partially seen on the artwork from Frazier’s version. Both artists have been working for Wizards of the Coast for quite some time now, but Frazier worked on the very first MTG set, Limited Edition Alpha, which was released in 1993.

Frazier’s artwork is featured on some of the most iconic Magic: The Gathering cards of all time, including Ring of Ma’rûf, Berserk, the first version of Swamp, and, of course, every single original Mox and Signet card. As a result, some fans assumed that the cards were merely very similar, and that Fraizer hadn’t intentionally plagiarized his fellow artist’s work.

As spotted by Polygon, this was reinforced by the fact that Frazier’s agent replied to a post on Facebook by fellow Wizards of the Coast artist, Donato Giancola, which stated: “After several changes requested we never saw this version.”

However, Wizards of the Coast and Frazier put out a joint statement on Bluesky this morning confirming that the similarities between the two artworks “are not coincidental.”

“I made a mistake, and I feel awful. I especially feel for Marta, whose work I adore,” reads Frazier’s part of the statement. “In trying to create an iconic version of The One Ring, while looking at references online, I ended up using Marta’s Ring as a reference and painted over it to try to depict the item the fans hold dear to their hearts. In doing so, I didn’t make it my own. I’m reaching out to Marta privately to apologize artist to artist.”

While Wizards of the Coast notes that Frazier’s mistake was not “intentional,” it did state that the company plans to compensate Marta Neal and credit her on “digital versions” of The One Ring card. Wizards of the Coast closed out the statement by saying, “Thank you to the eagle-eyed fans who noticed this, and for all of you for being strong advocates for amazing art and artists.”

The MTG maker said it still appreciates Frazier’s contributions. “This is a good moment to recognize that we are all humans who make mistakes,” the company wrote. “Dan made a mistake. We made a mistake in our process to not catch the error. We don’t condone or accept either, but we still value Dan and his contributions and are grateful for his place in the game.”



https://kotaku.com/one-ring-magic-the-gathering-artist-apologizes-after-fans-discover-plagiarism-i-made-a-mistake-and-i-feel-awful-2000692782
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Rumored Assassin’s Creed PvP Game’s Playtest Reportedly A Huge Letdown
item.content:encoded:
An upcoming PvP Assassin’s Creed with the codename “Invictus” has been rumored to be in the pipeline for years. Youtuber Jonathan, who has shared accurate leaks in the past, now claims to have been told about a private playtest for the spin-off held last month. The results, they claim, aren’t so great, leading to speculation that the negative feedback could imperil the game’s ongoing development as Ubisoft cuts projects right and left.

The alleged leaks compare Assassin’s Creed Invictus to Mediatonic’s slapstick mega-hit Fall Guys. Given the codename and the team behind it, For Honor’s Ubisoft Montreal, it should be assumed this translates to a series of Olympian-inspired qualification challenges with Creed’s signature parkour bonafides. Not so much zany jelly-beaned proportioned Altaïr’s being pancaked by giant mallets.

“I was told it was really fucking awful,” posted Assassin’s Creed streamer j0nathan (via GameRant). “It’s supposed to come out by the end of the year, but there’s a good chance it’ll get delayed or even canceled.” The streamer attached a screenshot from an Ubisoft playtest email to add some legitimacy.

Il y a eu des tests de Assassin's Creed Invictus le mois dernier (le 30 avril exactement) et on m'a rapporté que c'était vraiment nul à chier. Ça doit sortir en fin d'année mais y'a de grandes chances que ce soit reporté ou même annulé.

Si jamais vous y avez joué n'hésitez pas à… pic.twitter.com/OM1uouSVf2

— j0nathan (@xj0nathan) May 2, 2026

Projects in peril have become the norm at Ubisoft. While Assassin’s Creed Shadows was a much-needed hit and the recently revealed Black Flag remake is looking sharp, its witch trials-set sequel Hexe is experiencing behind-the-scenes turmoil after director Clint Hocking departed the company. A Reconstruction era Assassin’s Creed sequel was reportedly canceled by Ubisoft heads after reactionary backlash to Shadow’s leads and the American “political climate,” which further disappointed fans. The massive company is currently in the midst of a multi-year restructuring program that’s led to multiple rounds of layoffs and studio closures.

While multiplayer games have never been more scrutinized than now, it’s a shame to hear that Invictus could be DOA. Often simmering behind Ubisoft’s flagship projects, multiplayer-focused games like For Honor and Rainbow Six Siege have been highlights in the publisher’s history. Many players still take a shine to the cat and mouse PvP multiplayer modes introduced in 2010’s Brotherhood.



https://kotaku.com/rumored-assassins-creed-invictus-pvp-games-playtest-reportedly-a-huge-letdown-2000692801
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: “This Is Fine” Dog Fights For Overdue Therapy In Official Metroidvania
item.content:encoded:
In 2013, cartoonist KC Green set webcomics ablaze with a strip titled “On Fire.” In it, a bespoke cartoon dog (nicknamed “Question Hound”) enjoys a warm beverage while the world around him is aflame. “This Is Fine,” he says. And in the very interesting years since its debut, “This Is Fine” has become an international rallying call for getting through “it” day after day. Whether “it” is an emotional spiral or a foreshadowing of World War 3. Now, after a decade of anguish, Question Hound is receiving some long-overdue therapy in his first official video game, This Is Fine: Maximum Cope.

In this metroidvania-style platformer, Question Hound descends deep within his own psyche to conquer his major anxieties. Feelings of fear, failure, and regret manifest as worlds based around common sore spots, such as teenage failures, gym class humiliations, or workplace taboos. Each of these nightmare palaces is filled with nasty monsters, bosses, and puzzles.

Discovering new skills and fresh cups of coffee, you will help Question Hound overcome all these mental anguishes to find relief after 13 years in the making. Maximum Cope is like an aging millennial version of the Nightmare Ned game, a reference point you’d only know if you’re already an aging millennial.

Green’s involvement in the game’s development has been minimal beyond giving it his blessings. Speaking with him earlier this year for a zine, he preferred this arrangement, worried that his own creative impulse would have been to further punish Question Hound. Watching a cartoon animal go viral can be anguish in and of itself. Matt Furie’s Boys Club character Pepe infamously became the mascot for all kinds of online unpleasantness without involvement from his creator. Green felt similar pangs of concern whenever an official political party posted “This Is Fine” on their socials. However, learning from past mistakes and a couple of legal bottlenecks has allowed him to maintain control of this dog, sanctioning his adventures as he sees fit.

“We can all relate to ignoring the problem, hoping it goes away,” Green told me, “but this is a format that can let you conquer those anxieties.”



https://kotaku.com/this-is-fine-dog-fights-for-overdue-therapy-in-official-metroidvania-2000692724
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: PS5 Exclusive Marvel Tokon: Fighting Souls Adds Two More Avengers To Its Starting Roster
item.content:encoded:
The drip-feed of reveals for the spiritual successor to Marvel vs. Capcom continued this weekend at EVO Japan. Sony and developer Arc Systems took the opportunity to announce two new additions to the roster for Marvel Tokon: Fighting Souls, and they probably won’t be surprising anyone.

Savage Hulk and Shuri as Black Panther will be rounding out the Avengers side of the fighting game’s initial 20-character roster. It’s Savage Hulk because in Tokon, S.H.I.E.L.D. and Captain America have “exiled” Hulk to the Savage Land because of his “overwhelming power and instability.” Hence, the dinosaur bone armor. Hulk will have a Gamma Gauge that players build up during the course of a match to enter Gamma Rage mode and deploy extra powerful one-shot attacks.

Shuri’s backstory, meanwhile, has her only recently having taken on the mantle of Black Panther and become queen of Wakanda. Unlike Hulk, she’ll be fast and agile, with long range thanks to moves utilizing the Spear of Bashenga. She’ll also utilize a Bast’s Blessing mode to deash in multiple directions across the map to close the distance. I’m not sold on either character’s costume, but swappable skins, and potentially paid ones, will no doubt be part of the mix.

Joining Captain America and Iron Man, the Avengers side of the roster for Marvel Tokon is now complete. The Guardians and X-Men squads are too, leaving a number of villains on the table that could still be confirmed for the last six characters to be revealed. While Doctor Doom has been confirmed, Big bads like Thanos, Apocalypse, and Carnage are all still on the table. The end of the teaser trailer above certainly has fans hoping the Cletus Kasady/Symbiote duo is part of the mix.

Marvel Tokon is also getting four extra characters via DLC. Set to release on August 6, right after Spider-Man: Brand New Day and before Avengers: Doomsday, we’ll see if the remaining reveals end up tying into either of those tentpoles.



https://kotaku.com/ps5-exclusive-marvel-tokon-fighting-souls-adds-two-more-avengers-to-its-starting-roster-2000692787
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Ex-GTA Producer Takes A Break From MindsEye Mess To Help Someone Vibe-Code A Game
item.content:encoded:
Mark Gerhard and Leslie Benzies, the two co-CEOs of Build a Rocket Boy, recently decided to take some time out of their busy schedules, which usually consists of telling people they’re redundant via LinkedIn posts and allegedly using spyware to snoop on their employees, to “vibe-code” some new “in-game voice assistant” for MindsEye. 

Well, technically, ElevenLabs and the BARB co-CEOs actually helped vibe-code some nonsense for Arcadia, which is a “unique feature” within MindsEye. And by “unique feature,” I mean user-generated content. The AI-coded feature they managed to create–and by “they” I mean the AI–was “Arcadia Voice,” which is just… voice control. Did they really need an AI’s help for this? Congratulations, though, fellas, because now MindsEye can do something the Xbox Kinect could do back in 2010.

I vibe-coded a AAA game with the creator of GTA, the former CEO of Runescape (@laughivore), and their game studio’s Head of AI (@willgdjones).

We built an in-game voice assistant that understands the game state, can spawn weapons and objects, and even lets you create new objects… https://t.co/Xs2QRS0Gk6 pic.twitter.com/7TRemLD34K

— Joe Reeve – 🇬🇧/acc (@isnit0) April 30, 2026

However, there is one extremely impressive thing about ElevenLab’s video; it’s so unanimously hated that even the MindsEye community on Discord thinks it’s cringe. These are folks who actively enjoy the game MindsEye, by the way, so if they think this sucks, I think we may have truly reached the bottom of the barrel with this one.

In fact, the reaction from the 20 people who do still play the game was so terrible that Build a Rocket Boy’s new Head of AI, Will Jones, posted a lengthy statement on X in an attempt to quell the upset.

“We know that these will never replace the craft of developers, designers, artists, QA, or performers who care about what they are building (in fact, the coding agent is only useful because of the powerful system and beautiful assets already made by our colleagues),” reads part of Jones’ X post. “Games are creative, collaborative, and deeply human.”

Funnily enough, Jones also noted later in the statement that he is “new to game development,” due to the fact that Build a Rocket Boy is the first job he’s had in the gaming industry. However, I’d argue that Jones isn’t new to game development at all. In actuality, he’s new to asking an AI to develop video games for him. It would sort of be like if I said I’d got a new job in the culinary arts, because I’d just microwaved my very first ready meal.



https://kotaku.com/ex-gta-producer-takes-a-break-from-mindseye-mess-to-help-someone-vibe-code-a-game-2000692688
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
